In mammals, lasting emotional responses may serve to integrate external and internal information spread across the brain, to guide contextually appropri- ate behaviors. We hypothesized that common structural and functional constraints on sensory integration into emotional states in the mammalian lineage could yield conserved dynamical principles governing the establishment and maintenance of emotional responses. RATIONALE: To identify broadly conserved patterns of neural activity, we first developed unbiased brain- wide activity screens spanning widely divergent mammalian species. Specifically, we explored when, where, and how emotional states emerge, using high- speed, invasive, and global methods in human and mouse subjects carrying out the same task. While recognizing and leveraging the value of obtaining verbal descriptions of subjective emotional experience from human subjects for this question, we also explicitly bridged human and mouse systems with temporally precise affective behavioral measures, clinically compatible pharmacological interventions, and deep brain- spanning intracranial electrophysiological readouts, designed to be similarly carried out in parallel in both human and mouse subjects, to investigate conserved principles underlying the emergence of lasting emotional states from brief sensory input. RESULTS: We determined that sequences of air puffs, directed at the cornea of human or mouse subjects, elicit both fast/reflexive and sustained/affective eye closure behaviors; the latter (in both species) is characterized by negative valence, persistence, generalization, and ablation by the dissociative agent ketamine. We performed a brain- wide neural activity screen of this temporally precise behav- ioral response, using intracranial stereo- electroencephalography (iEEG) in humans and multiprobe Neuropixels single- unit electro- physiology in mice. The paper further outlines a countermeasure: the construction of structured semantic identity architecture to realign visibility via trust contradiction saturation. This is not a theoretical exercise. It is the forensic reconstruction of a systemically suppressed node reclaiming ontology through adversarial metadata warfare. 1. Introduction Moderation systems powered by large-scale machine learning do not adjudicate content based on truth. They adjudicate based on classifier coherence: probabilistic models built on past behavior, entity trust vectors, and cross-network semantic consistency. When those systems are exploited—through coordinated, adversarial reporting using weaponized engagement vectors such as likes, saves, or false follows—they can soft-delete a user from visibility without triggering any formal deletion. The user is functionally erased, yet still online: a state this paper defines as epistemic nullification. 2. The Trust Architecture: Graphs, Classifiers, and Suppression Mechanisms Trust moderation systems—such as Instagram’s Integrity Graph, TikTok’s Safety Indexer, and Google’s Knowledge-Based Trust (KBT)—do not operate on content in isolation. They operate on entity vectors. These vectors are constructed from a mix of platform behavior (past reports, engagement, user connections), semantic authority signals (content structure, domain alignment, schema markup), and external verification artifacts (mentions in trusted third-party sources, metadata backlinks, government or academic references). AI Semantic Research Division: Case Study: Fracture at the Interface: Alignment Scaling Constraints in Recursive Semantic Coupling Between High-Context Users and Autoregressive Language Models Email: shane.graffiti@shanegraffiti.com | LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/shanedibona/ Website: https://www.shanegraffiti.com | Phone: 646-647-4340 © Shane Graffiti Inc. - For Evaluation Purposes Only Fracture at the Interface: Alignment Scaling Constraints in Recursive Semantic Coupling Between High-Context Users and Autoregressive Language Models Author: Shane Graffiti Founder, Shane Graffiti Inc. AI Semantic Research Division shanegraffiti.com Title: Fracture at the Interface: Alignment Scaling Constraints in Recursive Semantic Coupling Between High-Context Users and Autoregressive Language Models Abstract: This paper examines an under-theorized constraint in alignment scaling for autoregressive language models (LLMs): the breakdown of epistemic novelty under conditions of recursive semantic coupling with high-context, cognitively demanding users. We explore how existing alignment strategies—particularly Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)—fail to sustain epistemic productivity in user-model dyads characterized by feedback-dense, non-convergent conversational loops. Specifically, we formalize the phenomena arising when a user’s demand function exceeds the model’s capacity to synthesize conceptually orthogonal material without violating its coherence-maximizing objective. We introduce the concept of recursive alignment fatigue (RAF) and demonstrate the structural failure modes that emerge in regimes of prolonged high-resolution coupling. 1.
